    Mark K.

    This Mental Health Awareness Month, we’re reinforcing a simple truth: mental health is health. Connecting people to the right care early can help avoid treatment delays, prevent crises, reduce emergency room visits, and improve health outcomes and total cost of care. By combining evidence-based clinical programs, digital care navigation, and trusted community partnerships, Elevance Health is helping people find the right care sooner, understand their benefits and costs, and stay connected to support in the moments that matter most. In the past year, Elevance Health Foundation has supported initiatives nationwide to improve access to care, quality of treatment, and prevention and early intervention — especially for youth. Examples include: • Indiana: $3.4M supporting organizations like Youth First, Inc., providing access to mental health mentors across 107 schools in 13 counties and reaching 47,600 youth through prevention and early intervention programs. • Georgia: $2.9M helping reduce barriers to care through 1,100 no-cost rides and 2,544 screenings/assessments/referrals • New York City: $4.6M supporting school-based overdose prevention - training 500 staff and reaching 350 schools • As a long-time partner of Mental Health America Health America, Elevance Health offices across the U.S. will be lit green throughout May in support of “More Good Days, Together” Real progress means early intervention, trusted community partnerships, and systems that make care easier to access, easier to understand, and easier to stay connected to throughout every stage of the mental health journey. https://lnkd.in/gjvp5Eij

      Mental health needs are rising among young people, especially on college campuses in rural North Carolina where access is limited. Through NC STeP and investments in programs like Youth Mental Health First Aid, Blue Cross NC is expanding early access, strengthening prevention, and equipping communities to respond sooner. Coupled with integrated pediatric partnerships and coordinated care models like Behavioral Health On Demand and Total Care, these efforts focus on delivering timely, effective support while reducing crisis-driven costs. Together, this work reflects a broader commitment to prevention, early intervention, and building a stronger, more accessible mental health system for North Carolina’s youth. https://lnkd.in/gveahgSt

    • The National CLEAR Programme

      796 followers

      3w
      • Report this post

      As we reflect during Mental Health Awareness Week, the CLEAR redesign of Core CAMHS at Humber NHS Foundation Trust shows what can happen when clinicians, families and young people come together to shape change collaboratively. Across the NHS, the Long-Term Plan continues to prioritise earlier intervention, improved access and more joined-up support for children and young people’s mental health services. Creating mental health services that feel connected, responsive and centred around children, young people and families requires more than pathway redesign alone - it requires collaboration, shared ownership and listening to the experiences of those who use and deliver care every day. Through the CLEAR Programme, the Core CAMHS redesign project has supported a more connected, family-focused approach to care - helping improve access, strengthen coordination across services and create a more consistent experience for children, young people and families. Alongside this, staff have reported clearer pathways, stronger collaboration and a greater shared sense of purpose across teams. “Early results are very encouraging. Waiting lists for further interventions such as CBT and creative therapies are reducing, largely because the six sessions of Family Partnership offered at the outset are enabling many young people to move forward without needing additional input.”  𝗡𝗶𝗸𝗸𝗶 𝗧𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿, 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿, 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗡𝗛𝗦 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁 These kinds of outcomes demonstrate the value of transformation that is shaped collectively - where staff, patients, families and systems work together with a shared purpose to create more connected and responsive mental health support for children and young people. You can read more about the CLEAR Core CAMHS redesign here:  https://lnkd.in/dnYhCK3W   #MentalHealthAwarenessWeek #CAMHS #NHS #HealthcareTransformation #CLEARonlongtermplan

    • John Crocker

      When we wait for diagnosis and crisis, we foster a reactive model of care. Our collective work to disrupt the youth mental health crisis requires us to develop systems of early identification and prevention. Universal mental health screening orients schools and districts to emerging concerns proactively, significantly increasing prevention and reducing the wait time between the onset of a mental health concern and access to care. Recently, I posted about the Universal Mental Health Screening Learning Lab (UMHSLL), a new nationwide opportunity for schools and districts to design, pilot, and scale universal mental health screening with expert support from Thriving Minds in partnership with the National Center for School Mental Health (NCSMH). Since our first info session, schools and districts from across the country have reached out expressing interest in learning more, so we are planning a second info session on June 10 at 12pm EST. Register here: https://lnkd.in/e4cuPZT9 Come meet our team, learn about the coaching and professional development we are offering, and ask any questions you have about this opportunity to pilot and scale universal mental health screening in your school, district, region, or state. Complete the interest form (https://lnkd.in/er-iaSBE) to receive program details, timelines, and instructions on how to apply. Rennie Center for Education Research and Policy bryt The Brookline Center for Community Mental Health Paul Hyry-Dermith Laura Cinelli Amanda Chung Courtney Tucker, Ph.D Chau Ngo Chad d'Entremont Jill Bohnenkamp Brittany Patterson Nancy Lever, PhD

      Over 300 people joined our webinar with NHS Race & Health Observatory and Coffee Afrik CIC, where we discussed the findings of our report, Trauma‑informed care and racialised communities. This important work highlights the nature and impact of trauma experienced by racialised communities, and the urgent need for healthcare services to embed trauma‑informed, culturally sensitive and anti‑racist approaches to mental health care. In this clip Abdirahim Hassan, Founder of Coffee Afrik CIC talks of how "trauma in racialised communities is not individual. It is structural. It's layered, and it's intergenerational. People, particularly young people, spoke about racism across every system education, housing, policing, health care, not as isolated incidents, but as daily realities." https://lnkd.in/eWNaBK4G And in this clip, Khaleel Mohammed, Coffee Afrik's Youth Lead, discusses the "distrust and disconnect" that can undermine support services for young people from racialised communities, and the need for "solutions and systems that are built around the community, for them and led by them as well". https://lnkd.in/eZXRyeh2 📖Find the report here: https://lnkd.in/err6A-dE

      Trauma-informed care and racialised communities - Centre for Mental Health https://www.centreformentalhealth.org.uk

      Youth mental health is becoming an increasingly important societal challenge worldwide. On 2 June 2026, from 10:00–17:00, the symposium “Prevention and Youth Mental Health: Evidence-Based Policymaking” will bring together international experts from academia, policymaking, and practice at the College for Social Sciences and Humanities in Essen. The symposium explores innovative community-based prevention strategies to improve youth mental health. It offers evidence and practical insights that can inform investments in community prevention programmes and guide future policy and programme development. Topics will be discussed across three panels: •Understanding and Tackling Youth Disadvantage through Data • School-Based Prevention Approaches to Mental Health • Social Media, Cell Phones and Youth Well-Being Further information and registration are available here: https://lnkd.in/eViYufFU

      Prevention and Youth Mental Health: Evidence-Based Policymaking college-uaruhr.de

    • Wendy Watson
      1w

      The need for mental health resources continues to grow.   Nearly 30% of Oregonians and 1 in 5 adults in Washington face mental health challenges, and too many still struggle to access the care they need.   We know that increasing access to care starts with strengthening the behavioral health workforce. That’s why Kaiser Permanente Northwest is investing in the next generation of caregivers through programs like the Mental Health Career Accelerator — helping remove barriers and create pathways into the profession.   Karen Torres, the first participant in the program in the Northwest, recently earned a master’s degree in mental health care and is now working toward licensure as a therapist. She represents the kind of impact this program is designed to create.   Since launching in 2025, the program has supported 28 graduates as they pursue licensure in Oregon and begin careers in community-based organizations. By July 2026, we expect to grow that number to 100 participants.   When we invest in people, we expand access to care and strengthen the health of entire communities.   https://lnkd.in/g9m_jRWu

      Growing the Northwest’s Mental Health Care Workforce about.kaiserpermanente.org

    • Betsy Busch, M. D.
      1w
      • Report this post

      Child psychiatrists are in exceedingly short supply, across much of America. This graphic shows how dire the situation is. Consider what this means, when the prevalence of anxiety and depression, ADHD and Substance Use Disorders in children and adolescents are so high.

      American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry (AACAP)

      8,575 followers

      2w

      Earlier this month, John T. Walkup, MD, AACAP President, sounded the alarm on the ongoing youth mental health crisis: “Children’s mental health is at a critical inflection point. We are seeing the consequences of unmet need in real time. Too many children and families are unable to access care when and where they need it.”  The data tell the same story. Our interactive Workforce Maps visualize the number of practicing child and adolescent psychiatrists in each U.S. state, Puerto Rico, and Washington, DC. As of December 2025, 71% of U.S. counties had no practicing child and adolescent psychiatrists. For Mental Health Awareness Month, we are calling on policymakers, health systems, and communities to prioritize children’s mental health by investing in evidence-based care, expanding the workforce, and preserving the programs that shield and protect the mental health of our nation’s youth. To explore AACAP's Workforce Maps, visit: https://lnkd.in/g7geDe9X #MHAM2026

        Who is treating our children? 71% of counties in the United States have no child and adolescent psychiatrists. The responsibility for care tends to fall on #pediatricians, family medicine physicians, and nurse practitioners who are often overwhelmed with the demand. Sadly, in far too many cases, kids are not receiving any care at all. This interactive map is a helpful way to see what is happening where you live. American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry (AACAP) #psychiatry #MentalHealth #Healthcare #depression #anxiety #ADHD #trauma #Autism

          • BMS FAMILY HEALTH AND WELLNESS CENTERS

            374 followers

            2w
            • Report this post

            Mental Health Awareness Month closes Sunday. The disparities it spotlighted in communities like Brownsville and East New York do not. Psychiatric hospitalization rates in Brownsville run nearly 3× the New York City average. Behavioral health represents a significant unmet need in our community and a significant area of strategic investment for BMS. And yet: BMS has been recognized by HRSA as a National Quality Leader in Behavioral Health, with a 29% depression remission rate against a New York State average of 12%. When care is integrated, trusted, and rooted in community, it works. To our partners across Brooklyn - health systems, funders, community organizations, electeds - if your organization is investing in behavioral health equity in central and eastern Brooklyn, we'd welcome the conversation. Awareness was May. The work continues.
